data.title: Vail Resorts: A Bad Snow Year Doesn't Change The Thesis
data.description:
Vail Resorts (MTN) has seen its share price punished by a lofty valuation along with the 2025-2026 ski season coming in as one of the worst in a decade+. Despite that, the company's intelligent moves for 2026-2027, along with an owned portfolio of top-tier ski assets, position Vail Resorts well to maintain its almost 7% dividend and strong shareholder returns.

Vail Resorts

Vail Resorts operates one of the most impressive portfolios in the ski industry, with 42 resorts and >2 million pass holders.

This massive integrated portfolio enables strong synergies as the company continues to work to build an integrated and profitable experience. At the same time, these assets are irreplaceable, with no major ski resorts built in decades. That means as demand grows, it'll be driven to Vail's existing resorts, improving margins in a high fixed-cost business.

Vail has continued to improve experiences despite the impact of the Park City Mountain labor union strike, one of the company's largest resorts, followed by a poor winter. This was a hurdle, but one that's past the company, and the net concessions from the union fight are not prohibitively expensive.

Vail has built the Epic Pass model with 2+ million Epic Pass holders driving 74% of visits and 64% of revenue in advance. That enables the company to handle season-to-season volatility better, with the $1 billion in Epic Pass revenue. That's incredibly key to an industry that has high fixed costs, and it's key to Vail's future success.

Vail Resorts Financial Results

Financially, the company's performance is seen below. Despite a 28% decline in snowfall and a 10% decline in visits, adjusted EBITDA stayed flat.

Similarly, the 2025-2026 season snowfall is incredibly weak, but we expect the company to be able to handle this. The company's resort reported EBITDA has stayed flat for the past few years, but the company has handled the impact of COVID-19 well. We expect Vail Resorts to be able to handle the weak 2025-2026 snowfall as well.

Vail Resorts has spent $2 billion in capital, $1.9 billion in dividends, and $0.9 billion in share repurchases over the past decade. That has put the company at a higher net debt position of ~$2.5 billion. At the same time, its share price decline since the above slide has pushed its dividend up to almost 7%.

Still, the company's dividend of less than $320 million on an annual basis is comfortably affordable. At the same time, with the Fed still expected to cut rates in coming years, that could help the company to manage interest expenses.

Vail Resorts Forecast

Vail Resorts is focused on continuing to increase revenue, and we think it's made some great decisions. In 2026-2027, pass offerings include a 20% discount for young adults aged 18-30, something that we think will attract new skiers to the resort and Epic Pass, based on anecdotal conversations with skiers and their families.

Epic has substantially increased pass revenue, even when accounting for some difficult winters, the impact of COVID-19, and the company's pricing reset. We expect the company to not grow at 16% annually on this front, but we do expect it to continue modest growth. Keep in mind, skiing is a high fixed cost industry, so reliable revenue here is key to the company's long-term success.

Still, skiing is ultimately a hobby, and a tough economy is a tough economy. We expect the pass to help Epic to be able to weather low snow years and lower visits, but what we expect it to struggle with is still a scenario such as the global financial crisis, where a recession causes people to substantially cut back on spending.

With skyrocketing oil prices threatening to cause such a recession, that is a major risk.

However, building loyalty among pass guests, especially given the low marginal costs for additional guests, has pushed down customer acquisition costs and substantially increased return rates. Those reliable commitments, customers who purchase a pass every year, are key to the company's future ability to provide reliable returns.

At the same time, Epic Pass has the ability to monetize auxiliary services. Only 6% of guests have taken ski school, and only 16% of guests currently rent with Vail Resorts. These auxiliary businesses provide just over $400 million in revenue, and they're both high-margin businesses. At the same time, on-mountain, especially for ski and ride school, there's no real competition.

That makes it a great opportunity for Epic Pass to grow these services, while providing value. Expansion in these services, given low existing market penetration, would be huge for future success. Again, it's worth noting that Vail Resorts has a high fixed-fee business, so any auxiliary growth here is all high margin.

Vail Returns

Vail has continued to generate strong FCF with a high conversion rate.

The company has moved to a more than 50% FCF conversion rate, maintaining high FCF. The company saw a substantial COVID-19 related dip; however, it has recovered substantially. We expect the company to be able to continue maintaining this FCF recovery. That'll enable Vail to maintain its dividend, invest in its business, and have strong returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that skiing is a luxury activity. With the potential of a global recession from higher oil prices, that could cause a drop-off in the company's performance, which could hurt its momentum. That could substantially hurt Vail's ability to continue driving shareholder returns, making it a poor investment.

Conclusion

Vail Resorts has an incredibly strong portfolio of assets in skiing. It's a nearly irreplaceable portfolio of assets, with no major new ski resort developments in decades. Vail's positioning has enabled the company to generate a strong cash flow yield, enabling an almost 7% dividend and strong performance from its portfolio.

At the same time, Vail Resorts has substantial opportunity. The Epic Pass is providing the company with reliable revenue in a high fixed cost business. At the same time, the company has substantial opportunity from lessons and gear rentals, which will provide the company with growing cash flow. That makes Vail Resorts a valuable investment.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 2025-2028 Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Objective Use for Morningstar category
Attractive risk-adjusted returns One-stop investment for 529 education savings Target-Enrollment 2027

Highlights

  • U.S. stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, although upward momentum slowed in the final quarter of the year.
  • The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI, and the Fed's interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December.
  • Healthcare, a laggard for much of 2025, was the top-performing sector in the S&P 500 Index during Q4.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 19.95% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 8.55% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net), a 45.00% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index and a 26.50% weighting in the Bloomberg 1-5 Year TIPS Index. Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Additionally, the portfolio's exposure to a hedged equity strategy detracted, as stock indexes moved higher, closing out a strong year. Conversely, Spectrum Income (T. Rowe Price) contributed modestly on a relative basis, largely due to our out-of-benchmark exposure to emerging-market bonds (both dollar-denominated and local currency). International Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by security selection in the financials sector. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.

data.title: Coeur Mining: Post-Acquisition Upside Remains Underappreciated
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered Coeur Mining (CDE), I upgraded them to a Strong Buy following the announcement of the New Gold acquisition, standing to benefit from complementary assets, diversified gold-silver production and strong cash flow for the remainder of this decade.

Following the completion of the New Gold deal, CDE remains a Strong Buy, guiding for even better than expected numbers and already delivering some improvements, while the valuation is now back to conservative levels thanks to what might turn out to be short-term macro pressure.

CDE-NGD Deal Now Complete

Following the recent closing of the New Gold [NGD] deal, Coeur provided an updated guidance and capital return program, with production expected to reach between 680,000 to 815,000 oz of gold, 18.7 to 21.9 million oz of silver and 50 million to 65 million lbs of copper, incorporating nine months of contributions from their 2 new mines in Canada, significantly above the 419,046 gold oz and 17.9 million silver oz seen in 2025 by Coeur alone, for a 65-30-5 percent commodity mix.

The company now expects to generate a free cash flow of $2 billion (with only 9 months of NGD mines contribution) with CAPEX reaching $500 million in 2026, and that’s based on solid consensus estimates as of October 2025, meaning $3,438/oz Au, $38.50/oz Ag and $4.50/lb Cu, which are nearly identical to the estimates I was going to use, saving us a bit of time in the valuation segment.

They also highlighted continued exploration and a two-year life extension at Rainy River, as well as New Afton’s solid expansion potential thanks to K-Zone, so they can certainly continue NGD's assets and definitely enjoy the major boost in cash flow they provide, as mentioned before. This is not over however, as they basically bought a major medium-term influx of cash flow that will have to be reinvested.

Financially, they now target a net cash position and also announced entering into a new $1 billion revolving credit facility (replacing the previous $400 million one) that should provide strong levels of liquidity - even stronger than what they already had. This is ultimately a result of the environment, and I’ll mention below that at worst, this bull run still allows miners to reach major achievements from several points of view.

As a result of the deal, the company also announced an expanded $750 million buyback program (~4% total yield; depending on when/if they finish it) - meaning 10 times higher than before - and a $0.02 semiannual dividend (~0.25% annual yield), which is not far from peers.

Near-Term Pressure May Not Last For Long

Although the market may have expected the ongoing Iran conflict to boost the demand for precious metals even more, the reality shows the opposite, with money fleeing into the dollar instead and artificially pushing these commodity prices lower.

This can also be boosted by the fact that last year’s jump was caused by the increase in demand coming from investing to the detriment of most other sources (banks down 21%, demand from jewelry down to 15-year lows), which can cause even more near-term volatility as speculative demand fades. Meanwhile, the Fed recently delayed rate cuts and only expects one later in 2026, while the ECB announced days ago that it’s ready to even hike rates even if the inflation surge is short-lived.

This also makes treasuries more and more attractive alongside stocks (with yesterday marking the worst day for the US market since the strikes against Iran began), with potential to see a rotation away from precious metals while causing even more demand for the dollar as a result - but that’s assuming that the Iran conflict would last for long, which I don’t believe to be the case, as detailed before here.

However, if this conflict de-escalates (with President Trump recently delaying his ultimatum for Iran by 10 days), there is potential to see the fundamentals take over again, with the market’s fear surrounding Japan’s potential US treasury sell-off, concerning levels of debt, political uncertainty/tariffs/etc. coming back into the spotlight, which is why I don’t believe this bull run is over.

The market simply ignored its need for a hedge for too long (since the pandemic, at least), and now it’s proven over and over again that it shouldn’t have, especially given the massive amounts of money printing. Similarly, Wells Fargo sees gold reaching $6,100 to $6,300/oz later in 2026 if these trends revert.

Another way to look at this historic bull run is that even if prices revert to much more conservative levels, many of these companies are now stronger than ever, with massive amounts of cash in many cases, much cleaner balance sheets, being ready to develop or even continue M&A activity regardless of gold prices. Meanwhile, others, such as Caledonia (CMCL), even secured major chunks of their production at gold prices above $3,500/oz for the next nearly 3 years, with this brief bull run (assuming it doesn’t recover) still being an immense boost for many of them.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, gold and silver prices will dictate this company’s performance both from a business and a stock perspective, and that should always be accounted for when talking about miners.

For CDE in particular, the hard part is far from over now that they bought New Gold, as the company will have to invest massive amounts of money efficiently going forward since they bought assets with relatively short life of mines - unless they want to distribute most of it back to shareholders and scale down, which may not be the best move.

Valuation

For Coeur’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll start with their estimated FCF of $2 billion, taking into account consensus prices of $3,438/oz Au, $38.50/oz Ag and $4.50/lb Cu. Note that this includes only 9 months of contributions from NGD during what I highlighted before as being their peak year, but I’ll use it as a base since it can help us highlight NGD’s production downfall until 2030.

To take into account their potential to grow significantly using this massive cash flow boost (even 3-4% yields from holding treasuries on this much money adds interest that would provide a similar CAGR, at worst), I’ll assume a flat FCF until 2030, followed by another drop of $250 million in 2031 due to NGD’s expected drop (although mine life extension can help alleviate this) and a 4% CAGR from that point until 2035.

I’ll use a terminal growth rate of 2.5% from this point, with a discount rate of 11% to take into account the execution risk since we’re talking about a company that will have to invest quite a bit in the following years to repurpose the influx of capital from New Gold’s assets, with 1.034 billion shares outstanding following the deal.

This way, we get to an equity value of $20.21 billion, meaning an intrinsic value of $19.55 per share, which is still above the current $16.17, while the company has significant room to deliver better performances than this.

Conclusions

CDE remains a Strong Buy following the completion of the New Gold deal, adding a massive influx of cash flow that they can reinvest into significant levels of growth going forward.

As mentioned, there are certain risks to keep in mind, but the current valuation continues to be conservative given gold’s long-term potential, in my opinion, especially if the Iran conflict de-escalates quickly and recent trends revert.

data.title: The End Of Bitcoin Will Be Its New Beginning
data.description:
The “maximalist” system of Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) seems to be collapsing. The mathematical models that perfectly described Bitcoin’s value based on its scarcity are failing to describe the price trend.

But in the end, isn’t scarcity exactly what gives value to this asset?

And if scarcity no longer matters, will it be the end of Bitcoin?

The way I see it, it could potentially be a new beginning.

In my opinion, there is a very specific reason why models based on scarcity are failing in their purpose. A reason to be found elsewhere. But the value of “scarcity” has not disappeared; it is only unexpressed. And it may be accumulating to explode when we least expect it.

Here is my thesis.

The collapse of canonical models

For years, the price of Bitcoin has been driven by a maximalist narrative supported by some mathematical models, now considered canonical. Models that have been able to describe the price trend of Bitcoin since the first halving are models that over time have adapted and evolved. Models on which the “maximalist” thesis has built a religion but that today seem to begin to give way.

Stock-to-flow

The first and most renowned is stock-to-flow, which measures the value of Bitcoin in relation to scarcity. The same logic that drives gold: today, gold’s S/F is 60, while that of BTC-USD is 113, so Bitcoin even has a more pronounced controlled scarcity mechanism than gold. A model that until 2022 described Bitcoin’s trend well but that today is instead making a huge miss, and it does not surprise me that this model is talked about less and less.

And this has happened in parallel with the progressive abandonment of the parallelism between Bitcoin and the concept of a “store of value” of the future. Not surprisingly, a divergent trend has been generated compared to gold, even though the underlying narrative that made it “viral” was precisely that of “digital gold.” (We will see why later).

HPR

It stands for Halving Price Regression, which is the non-linear regression built exclusively on Bitcoin prices on halving days. What everyone calls the rainbow chart. It should be an adaptive evolution of price, always based on its progressive reaction to the increase of the “scarcity” variable, which theoretically is what makes Bitcoin “unique” in this sense, as well as (in my honest opinion) what should drive long-term demand. But here too, the model has failed, or rather is failing, in describing the evolution of the market, and the cycle remains anchored to the lower part of the rainbow.

The model that best describes it

In short, Bitcoin’s relationship with scarcity has weakened, and that’s a fact. Why? And why today, with the conflict in Iran, is this consideration a central element for Bitcoin?

The high beta of the Nasdaq-100

From “digital gold,” the abandonment of models linked to scarcity has also brought it closer, even at the level of media narrative, to the Nasdaq-100/S&P 500. In fact, the correlation has increased to the point of becoming, in my opinion, a sort of high-beta asset of the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100.

It amplifies upward movements, and also downward ones, typical of high-beta assets.

Why is this shift happening?

Because the truth that no one wants to accept is that, scarcity or not, the price of Bitcoin follows ONLY its demand. And demand, in turn, does not depend on scarcity. It has probably never been that way…

This is why this is important

Thinking, therefore, that halving or some mathematical model is enough to describe the “needs” of demand is, in my opinion, pure myopia. And the reason why BTC’s performance has not followed the canonical evolution that instead described past halving cycles is clear, in plain sight: a completely different monetary environment from that of the last 15 years.

Interest rates are 4x higher than those of the last halving cycle and at least 2x higher than the average of the last 15 years. And if the cost of money increases, the real engine that drives the expansion of “scarce” assets, namely the M2 money supply in circulation, decreases.

The conflict in Iran

And here a problem comes in: the conflict in Iran has pushed the 1Y break-even inflation (BEI) above 5%. The yield curve has shifted upward, making expectations about the cost of money (mainly in the short term) higher than in the past.

If the short-term cost of money increases, the liquidity that the Fed injects into the system and Trump’s fiscal stimulus will have a less significant impact on the “concept of scarcity” of Bitcoin. And don’t make the mistake of saying, “Then it should have done the same with gold.” Saying that would mean ignoring that the surge in the price of gold has been the result of abnormal purchases by central banks. Bitcoin is not subject to this same institutional flow as of today. Therefore, it naturally follows dynamics much closer to the money market.

Is Bitcoin dead?

So in the short term, it is natural that Bitcoin has reacted progressively worse since January, when BEI started to increase. Regardless of the fact that it “had not finished the halving cycle.” And in my opinion, it will continue to suffer as long as inflation expectations, and therefore monetary expectations, continue to be weighed down by the conflict in Iran and thus by the price of energy.

And so, if high rates are expected for long and scarcity stops being a value, has the end come for Bitcoin? We could also build a thesis in this direction.

Risk

Behind Bitcoin, there is an industry: mining. This industry is driven by mining farms. The revenues of mining farms depend on Bitcoin difficulty and, therefore, on the hashrate. Today, with the price falling, and after years in which mining farms have invested heavily, they find themselves in a market with a very high hashrate. They are tech companies, and often mid-cap, which in theory makes them sensitive to interest rate trends. If difficulty remains high, with the price falling, and rates are high, mining farms could be forced to sell Bitcoin, and this would generate selling pressure that would be difficult to manage in the short term, in theory.

If a degenerative loop were to form, a whole structure could actually collapse. And yet …

My opinion

The short term, as usual, blinds the investor who looks at the long term.

Looking at the 5- or 10-year BEI, such anomalous levels are not yet visible. In other words, the market believes that the impact of the energy shock on inflation is transitory (less than 5 years at least). An element that, combined with a latent cycle of non-monetized scarcity, “cleaning” of less profitable mining firms, and positive monetary and fiscal expansion … would in turn create a huge gap in the market to be filled for Bitcoin.

In other words, new mining companies would enter the industry and fill the gap (arbitrage) that has been created.

So yes… in the short term, I think Bitcoin has to price in the weight of a different economic environment, and scarcity will not save it, but also that the underlying model that drives the cryptocurrency industry remains embedded in a long-term bullish cycle.

Attention… not for “maximalist” reasons or blind trust. For me, Bitcoin has no use. But because of how the industry has developed, which in fact is not so different from that of gold.

Buy the dip?

In practical terms, for me, these lows are to be bought only if the investment horizon is long enough to get through the short-term tightening cycle, which, according to the market (judging by BEI) today, is around 5 years. In the short term, monetary conditions do not, in my opinion, favor BTC-USD, which, as I always like to remind you, has often recorded drawdowns of up to 80% in past cycles. Expanding the time horizon, however, it becomes an opportunity because a combination of positive factors is created: monetary easing, unexpressed scarcity at the monetary level, and new institutional flows (ongoing).

Conclusion

I think the right rating for Bitcoin today is HOLD. In the short term, I think it still has to price in the weight of the new economic conditions. But in the long term, an interesting gap in valuations may emerge.

data.title: Graphite Miners News For The Month Of March 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the March edition of the graphite miners news.

The past month saw China battery related flake graphite spot prices mixed, but generally flat. Prices remain very depressed.

March saw a really positive month of progress for many of the graphite junior miners.

Graphite price news

During the past 30 days the China graphite flake-194 EXW spot price was flat. The China graphite flake +195 EXW spot price was flat. Note that 94-97% is considered best suited for use in batteries; it is then upgraded to 99.9% purity to make “spherical” graphite used in Li-ion batteries. The spherical graphite 99.95% min EXW China price was up 4.70% the past 30 days. Note: Graphite electrode prices were generally flat.

Note: SMM lists the current China flake-194 graphite spot price at US$346/t.

Graphite demand and supply forecast charts

The IEA Global critical Minerals Outlook 2024 report forecasts graphite demand and supply pipeline to 2040 (source)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

2021 IEA forecast growth in demand for selected minerals from clean energy technologies by scenario, 2040 relative to 2020 - Increases Of Lithium 13x to 42x, Graphite 8x to 25x, Cobalt 6x to 21x, Nickel 7x to 19x, Manganese 3x to 8x, Rare Earths 3x to 7x, And Copper 2x to 3x

Graphite market news

In March Fastmarkets reported: "US duties solidify, prices stagnant post-Lunar New Year."

data.title: Navan Q4 Review: Relief Rally Creates Exit Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of Navan, Inc. (NAVN) have been a poor performer since its 2025 IPO, with the stock falling from $25 to ~$8. Navan is a perfect example of a low-profit, high-revenue growth stock using AI to enhance its business, which has come under pressure in recent months as market sentiment has softened. However, on Thursday, shares soared over 40% in a relief rally following better-than-expected quarterly results. Now is a good time to see if this was simply a reaction to oversold conditions or if this rally can continue further.

Navan is disrupting the corporate travel industry

Navan uses an AI-powered platform to help companies’ employees manage corporate travel expenses and make payments all on one platform. This makes it easier for enterprises to manage their overall corporate travel spending, and it also reduces employees’ time in booking their travel, freeing up more of their time to do work on their actual job. In this way, Navan’s value proposition to enterprises is enabling them to both more easily manage their spending and improve employee productivity. It estimates that its offering saves ~15% vs. legacy models with a booking time on average that is less than 10 minutes.

The corporate travel industry is a competitive one. There are large, entrenched players like SAP SE's (SAP) Concur and American Express Company’s (AXP) GBT, among other platforms. Clearly, though, its AI agent approach is working. 90% of bookings are done on the platform, and the average time to book a trip is just 7%. Agent interactions generated the same customer satisfaction score as peers with human agents. This is now generating much better financial results. In the company’s fiscal fourth quarter, Navan earned $0.02, much better than the $0.24 feared loss, as revenue surged 35% to $178 million.

Margin improvement is encouraging

Most impressively, gross margins expanded 400 bps to 72%, driving a ~42% increase in gross profit to $128 million. The company has dramatically improved its margin as it has scaled up over the past two years, going from substantial losses to modest profitability. I would note that its adjusted operating margin excludes share-based compensation (“SBC”); otherwise, the business is still loss-making. Nonetheless, the trajectory is unambiguously positive.

Now, Navan gets 90% of its revenue from commissions paid by suppliers on its platforms (airlines, hotels, etc.), with just 10% tied to subscription revenue paid by enterprise clients. Because it primarily earns revenue from transaction activity, its business is more cyclical than technology companies with a focus on recurring license fees. That does leave growth exposed to any pullback in corporate travel activity. Thus far, the company is seeing limited impact from the Iran conflict, with just low single-digit exposure to the Middle East.

I remain a bit cautious about whether this can persist. While it has limited direct exposure, a prolonged period of elevated inflation could slow growth and lead businesses to reduce discretionary spending activity. In general, curtailing travel is one of the easiest ways for companies to trim spending, and I believe this could be a headwind for growth. The magnitude depends on the duration of the energy market disruption, which is unknowable at this time.

Business mix has not improved

Gross booking value (GBV) jumped 42% to $2.3 billion. For the full year, bookings rose 38%. One thing to note is that booking volumes grew faster than revenue, given pricing compression. This is something that is likely to continue, with management expecting a 30 bp compression in yield during fiscal 2027. Interestingly, bookings rose faster than payment volumes. Over the medium term, NAVN wants to begin capturing payment activity of its customers to earn value across the entire transaction chain and increase recurring revenue. While it is winning more bookings, it is struggling to convert enterprises to its card. That development bears watching.

Separately, in January, Navan announced plans to roll its Reed & Mackay business under its Navan brand. Navan acquired this UK business, which focuses on higher-touch, premium services for executives, in 2021, but it has operated as a standalone brand. While operating one brand makes sense, it will be important during the integration not to disrupt the quality of service that Reed & Mackay customers have come to expect; otherwise, it risks increased attrition. A successful integration should, in my view, open the door to incremental growth by offering customers using its larger Navan platform a premium service option, expanding the potential addressable market.

Share-based compensation is a cost to monitor

Finally, in Q4, the company turned free cash flow positive with ~$30 million of free cash flow. Now, I would note that the company has $47 million of share-based compensation. While a noncash expense, this is a real expense for shareholders given the dilution from the equity issuance. Adjusting for this, free cash flow was still negative. Many technology companies have large share-based expenses, which can inflate their apparent free cash flow, and investors should consider this expense closely in their valuation assessment, in my view.

Navan has a strong balance sheet, which is important as it is still scaling the business to achieve consistently positive free cash flow. It ended the quarter with $616 million of net cash. That gives it plenty of financial flexibility to consider bolt-on M&A or fund further tech development. Eventually, it could use cash to buy back stock and offset the significant dilution from share-based compensation, but I do not view that as likely in calendar year 2026.

Alongside the results, the company offered encouraging guidance. It expects revenue to grow about 30% in Q1 to $205 million, while full-year revenue should be $866-$874 million, or about 24% growth. It is targeting about $60 million of operating income, implying a ~7% margin. I view this outlook as credible, barring a worsening of global travel conditions. While the outcome in Iran is unknown, I fear disruption may be greater for longer, so I expect results to be in the bottom portion of the range, given its reliance on transaction revenue.

This implies about $0.20-$0.23 of operating income/share. However, including share-based compensation, I expect operating income to still be negative in fiscal 2027. In the past few months, we have seen markets punish software companies that have excessive share-based compensation, and valuation screeners that do not account for this expense can make stocks look less expensive than they truly are. In this case, NAVN is likely 18-24 months from being profitable after SBC while also having exposure to travel levels.

Investors should exit NAVN

There also remains the question of whether its use of AI leads to a permanent advantage, or if its competitors, like AXP, that have substantial financial capacity, can develop similar tools. NAVN has proven to be an effective disruptor with ~5% market share, but the durability of its advantage is uncertain and likely to cap valuation, especially with AI tools improving at such a rapid pace, which means that technological advantages can be short-lived.

While the company has improved margins, we are unlikely to see “true” profitability after SBC until fiscal 2029, in my view. Particularly with risks in corporate travel skewed to the downside, I view this rally as an opportunity to exit shares. Their beaten-up nature made them prone to a bounce, which has now happened, creating a better selling point. I view NAVN as a highly speculative stock, and in this market environment, I am cautious about businesses that are not generating underlying profits. As such, I view NAVN as a “sell” and would exit.

data.title: What AI's Threat Might Do To The March 2026 Job Report
data.description:
On April 3, 2026, the Bureau of Labor Statistics will report non-farm payrolls for March. The data might show the impact of the oil crisis on the labor market. The chances are very low that employment will rise like it did in February.

In February, employment trended higher in five main areas: healthcare, financial activities, transportation/warehousing, and social assistance. Jobs in the Federal government fell.

Job Loss Shocker in February

The BLS reported that total nonfarm payroll employment edged down by 92,000 in February. Despite the large figure, the unemployment rate of 4.4% did not change by much. Compared to the year before (in February 2025), the unemployment rate held a range of between 4.0% and 4.2%. Unfortunately, the upcoming March job data might come in weaker for several reasons.

In chart 1 (below, left), the unemployment rate headed toward, but stayed below, 4.5%. Last November, the rate peaked after the BLS did not collect data in October. The downtrend ended in February, when job losses increased.

In chart 2, the BLS posted a dramatic swing of over 100,000 jobs created in January and a drop the month after. Even though the Supreme Court ruled against various Liberation Day 2025 tariffs, a hangover might persist. Higher oil prices compound that problem, affecting trade. Reuters reported that US import prices posted the biggest increase in four years amid a broad rise in goods.

After Iran blocked the Strait of Hormuz in response to the U.S. and Israel's actions, an oil crisis ensued. WTI crude rose from around $65 to around $100. It settled below $91 at the time of writing this article.

The oil prices most affected are those that depend on ships unable to pass the Strait.

The longer the conflict lasts, the greater the impact that higher energy prices will have on the economy beyond a few weeks. Consumers will cut spending as prices rise. Companies will cut jobs to lower their expenses. Technology firms like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), ASML Holding N.V. (ASML), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) will cut staff while committing to growing capital expenditures.

In the establishment data for February, the job decline in professional and business services was 2,000, led by a 9,300 drop in professional, scientific, and technical services. In March, job declines potentially accelerated. Technical consulting services add jobs, softening the decline. However, Gartner, Inc. (IT), Fair Isaac Corporation (FICO), Accenture plc (ACN), and Thomson Reuters Corporation (TRI) are on a sustained year-long downtrend. This weak performance suggests that markets are pricing in AI to replace staff this year.

Increase in Healthcare Jobs

The aging U.S. population will continue to create a high demand for health care jobs. That resulted in a 77,000 job increase in January. In February, health care jobs fell by 37,000. The BLS blamed strike activity for the drop. Despite the broken trend, expect hospitals, health care services, and nursing and residential care facilities to hire staff. AI cannot replace them.

Transportation and warehousing fell by 11,000. Couriers and messengers saw a 17,000 drop in employment. Air transportation added 5,000 to minimize the drop. Again, higher energy costs would raise parcel delivery prices at the U.S. Postal Service, United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS), and FedEx Corporation (FDX). That might pressure firms to cut jobs in March.

Profit margins will face pressures from high energy prices. TFI International Inc. (TFII), Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (CP), XPO, Inc. (XPO), and Old Dominion Freight Line, Inc. (ODFL) gave up recent gains after the Middle East war broke out. Investors will have a better idea of the headwinds in staff additions in this industry from next week’s jobs report.

Government Job Cuts

A modest drop in government jobs likely continued, especially as severance payments ended. It fell by 10,000 in February and is down by 11% (-330,000) since its peak in October 2024. As I repeatedly wrote in past job preview reports, the departments related to defense and procurement of military equipment will not lose jobs.

The threat from the government to limit bonuses or executive salaries briefly hurt shares of RTX Corporation (RTX), L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX), Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT), and Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC) last fall. Since then, shares have risen to new highs. They peaked on March 3.

Stocks that dipped by more included Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc. (KTOS) and AeroVironment, Inc. (AVAV). Conversely, Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) traded in a range in the last month. Here are the peers to KTOS stock:

Seeking Alpha quant factor grades below warned of the overvaluation in AVAV stock, despite the recent drop.

Both RTX and KTOS have attractive EPS revisions and momentum. Lockheed has a strong profitability grade, while Rocket Lab scored well on growth, momentum, and EPS revisions.

Wage Increase

March’s wage growth needs to keep up with the prior month. In February, the average earnings rose by 0.4%, or 4.8% annualized. The average increase in the past 12 months is 3.8%. The consumer will likely brace for a Consumer Price Index surge in March and for a few months afterward. They need income growth to keep up with inflation. The CPI increased by 0.3% in February and by 2.4% over the last 12 months.

Investors sold consumer discretionary stocks on worries that company profits would worsen. In 2026, The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) gained around 17% before falling back. Compare PG stock to its peers. The SA Quant Rating is a hold for P&G, The Coca-Cola Company (KO), Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL), and The Clorox Company (CLX). Only Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) and Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR), which posted 9% Y/Y revenue growth, have a buy rating.

Coca-Cola stock has a strong set of grades. Although P&G and most of the others score a D- on valuation, investors seeking defense should pick stocks with strong profitability grades.

Your Takeaway

The market will grow increasingly concerned about jobs after the January BLS data. Health care jobs consistently increased monthly, except in February. However, the aging U.S. economy created strong demand for healthcare jobs. AI continues to threaten office jobs where the work is repetitive. The Magnificent 7 companies seeking operating cost cuts to buy AI hardware will cut positions that AI might automate. While automation is nothing new, rapid advances in AI will put pressure on the job-hiring data.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CASH: Reasonable Place To Park Your Cash
data.description:
Introduction

Since its launch in late 2021 Global X High Interest Savings ETF (CASH:CA) has grown into one of Canada's favorite spots to park their cash. It currently has $6.8B in assets, growing from $4.8B two years ago. Its popularity was aided by rising interest rates and yield curve inversion (lower short-term interest rates higher than longer-term rates). Now that Canada has reduced policy interest rates and the yield curve has normalized, I'll compare CASH:CA to other low-risk, fixed income options.

What CASH Holds

Unlike money market funds such as ZMMK:CA which hold short-term treasury bills and commercial paper, CASH:CA holds its assets in high-interest deposit accounts at Canadian chartered banks. Currently, assets are held at the National Bank of Canada (NA:CA), Scotiabank (BNS:CA) and CIBC (CM:CA).

According to CASH's prospectus, it may also invest in "high-quality, short-term (one year or less) debt securities, including treasury bills and promissory notes issued or guaranteed by Canadian governments or their agencies, and banker's acceptances" but its most recent holdings show it does not currently hold any of these investments.

Fees

CASH has a Management Expense Ratio (MER) of 0.11%. This compares favorably to other high-interest savings ETFs such as Purpose High Interest Savings (PSA:CA) and CI High Interest Savings (CSAV:CA) which have MERs of 0.17% and 0.15%, respectively. It's also comparable to money market funds such as ZMMK:CA, which has an MER of 0.13%.

Liquidity

Liquidity is not an issue for CASH. Average daily trading volume over the last 12 months is 1,576,231, with large volumes on the bid and ask and a bid/ask spread of $0.01.

Risk

Unlike when you open a high-interest savings account yourself or invest in Guaranteed Investment Certificates (GIC) your investment in CASH is not covered by the Canada Deposit Insurance Corporation (CIDC) or any other government deposit insurer. This means CASH is subject to counterparty risk at the banks they make their deposits at. The banks that CASH deposits money at are well capitalized and some of the largest in Canada, so the risk is small but important to be aware of.

Current Distribution and Yield

CASH pays monthly distributions with ex-dividend dates near the end of each month, with payments coming approximately a week later. The most recent distribution was February's, which came in at $0.071/ share.

CASH typically trades at $50 a share with regular drops in price each month after the ex-dividend date, followed by climbs as interest accrues over the month.

The most recent distribution works out to an annualized distribution yield of 1.7%, which is a sharp drop (27% decrease) from 2.33% when using the distribution from January 2026. As there hasn't been a drop in Canada's policy interest rate since October 2025 I was surprised by the decrease in distribution. Looking back to February of 2024 something similar, just to a lesser degree, happened as well. The distribution dropped by 10% without any change in Canada's policy interest rate.

Typically the yield of CASH follows closely with that of the Canadian policy rate. To show this, I plotted the annualized yield for CASH and the Canadian Overnight Repo Rate Average (CORRA) each month since Jan 2023. CORRA closely tracks the policy rate but represents actual market transactions. Looking at the chart, it looks like there's a chance that future distributions increase from February's payments of $0.071. If CASH's annualized yield spread compared to CORRA reverted back to what it was over the last 12 months, then CASH's annualized yield would be 2.07%. We'll use both 1.7% and 2.07% when comparing to other short-term fixed-income alternatives.

Comparison of Alternatives

In 2023 and 2024 Canada's yield curve was inverted, and the yield you got from short-term fixed-income investments like CASH was as high or higher than longer-term alternatives. In essence, you weren't getting paid for the duration risk you were taking with longer-duration alternatives. Fast forward to today, and the yield curve has normalized, providing higher yields for longer-duration investments.

The question becomes, how much yield are you willing to sacrifice in order to take no duration risk (buy CASH)? To get an idea of the amount of yield we're leaving on the table, I put together the below comparison of alternatives. CASH has the lowest yield of the options presented (2.07%, assuming the best case that future distributions normalize to the historical CORRA spread). ZMMK, a money market fund, pays a slightly higher yield (2.28%) while still taking on low duration risk (most holdings mature in <180 days) and being highly liquid.

Saven offers a 2.85% yielding savings account, though it's extra work to set up a new account and the interest rate could change at any time.

GICs offer higher yields, with yields increasing out to a year but then leveling off. If you're willing to lock your money up for a year, you can increase your yield by 76% (3.65% vs. 2.07%).

Option Yield Source
CASH:CA 1.7% / 2.07% Annualized Last Distribution / Historical Spread to CORRA
ZMMK:CA 2.28% Annualized Last Distribution
High-Interest Savings Account 2.85% Saven
3 Month GIC 2.60% EQ Bank
6-Month GIC 2.75% EQ Bank
9-Month GIC 2.85% EQ Bank
1-Year GIC 3.65% WealthOne
3-Year GIC 3.70% WealthOne
5-Year GIC 3.85% EQ Bank

Conclusion

CASH is a popular ETF providing a reasonable yield for a highly liquid, zero-duration-risk, fixed-income product. For those willing to sacrifice some liquidity to gain significantly more yield, a 1-year GIC looks like a reasonable choice.

Another alternative is to build a GIC ladder using CASH, 3-month, 6-month, 9-month, and 1-year GICs. This way you'll have capital available for any short-term opportunities and additional influxes of cash coming every 3 months.

data.title: ECB Rate Hikes? Not So Fast
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

There is renewed excitement in financial markets about how the ECB might react to the war in the Middle East, with markets now pricing in more than three rate hikes. Needless to say, in such uncertain times, almost anything is possible, and nothing can be entirely ruled out. The real question, however, is whether markets have looked beyond the headlines and taken on board the messages from Wednesday’s ECB Watchers Conference, including remarks by Philip Lane and Christine Lagarde.

At least in my own humble opinion, those remarks point to a somewhat different reaction function than what markets are now pricing. After last week’s ECB meeting and press conference, it was already obvious that the central bank had made a hawkish pivot and would remain vigilant, arguably more vigilant than in 2022. Back then, however, the ECB was emerging from an extremely accommodative stance and normalising policy from negative interest rates and quantitative easing. With hindsight, the biggest policy mistake was probably the delayed response to an energy price shock that ultimately morphed into a broader inflation surge. Learning from that episode though does not mean a rate hike is imminent.

Lagarde’s speech showed that the ECB, like the rest of us, is currently thinking about a range of different scenarios. As long as the energy price shock remains broadly contained, including first‑round knock‑on effects, it's far from certain that the ECB will react at all. For rate hikes to come back onto the table, the Bank would need to see a rise in inflation expectations and a broadening of inflationary pressures across the economy. So far, the war in the Middle East has instead weighed on business and consumer confidence. Meanwhile, the labour market is entering this energy shock in a weaker position than in 2022, and governments’ fiscal pockets are more constrained, making large‑scale stimulus to offset higher energy prices less likely.

What does this imply for the ECB’s reaction function? While the Bank will continue to update its scenarios, it will be the actual developments, rather than forecasts, that determine the ECB's next steps. The ECB is back to a “driving at sight” approach. Key variables to watch are actual inflation data, survey‑based longer‑term inflation expectations, and wage developments, all of which will be weighed against the risk of slowing economic activity and financial stability concerns.

Looking ahead to the ECB meeting at the end of April, there will be no new forecasts, and only limited data available: March inflation, a handful of country inflation releases for April, and initial estimates of first‑quarter GDP on the day of the meeting. In all honesty, that does not look sufficient to move the needle, unless the ghosts of 2022 are really keeping policymakers awake at night.

For the June meeting, the story is different. Simply put, if we are still talking about the war in the Middle East and high energy prices by then, a rate hike is clearly possible. However, this is not our base case, as we expect the Strait of Hormuz to reopen before then.

In short, our view of the ECB’s reaction function has not changed in recent days. We think the ECB – like us – is expecting an initial inflation wave, starting with gasoline prices, followed by knock-on effects on transportation costs, food prices and other industrial products. As long as this remains a single, time‑limited wave, there is no need for ECB rate hikes. Even though it’s a forbidden word in Frankfurt, some might call this ‘transitory’.

That said, three potential pain points remain for the ECB: a psychological one, ie headline inflation above 4%, reviving uncomfortable memories of 2022; an analytical one, ie core inflation above 3%, signalling broader price pressures; and a credibility one, ie a surge in survey‑based inflation expectations, which would make inaction increasingly difficult to justify.

In today’s environment, almost anything is possible, even ECB rate hikes, but that's still not our base case.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Rates Tick Higher As Housing Demand Grows
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Mortgage rates continue to rise, but housing market is gradually improving. (00:14) Netflix (NFLX) hiking subscription plans to pay for $20B content budget. (01:05) Senate advances Homeland Security funding bill as shutdown strains airports. (01:58)

This is an abridged transcript.

Freddie Mac's chief economist says, "The housing market continues to show gradual improvements compared to a year ago amid recent rate volatility.” Sam Khater noted, “Purchase and refinance applications are up year-over-year, and rates remain lower than last year.”

But rates have increased week over week.

The latest Freddie Mac (FMCC) Primary Mortgage Survey showed

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.38% as of March 26, up from 6.22% last week but below 6.65% in the same period a year ago.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.75%, up from 5.54% last week and down from 5.89% a year ago.

Your next Netflix bill will be higher.

Netflix (NFLX) is raising the price of all its subscription plans by at least a dollar.

The company telegraphed the increase during its earnings call in January.

Netflix says it needs the extra money to help pay for the company’s higher content budget, expected to rise to $20B this year. That’s a $2B increase. This includes a slate of live events, including the World Baseball Classic in Japan, podcasts, and a new license deal with Sony (SONY).

Effective immediately, a standard plan with ads will cost $8.99 a month (+$1), standard with no ads will cost $19.99 a month (+$2), and premium will cost $26.99 a month (+$2). The company also raised its extra member fee by a dollar to $7.99 a month (with ads) and to $9.99 a month (without ads).

Netflix (NFLX) last raised its subscription fees in January 2025.

President Trump said he plans to sign an order to pay Transportation Security Administration (TSA) officers immediately.

This comes at the same time the Senate passed legislation to fund most of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

But that bill still needs approval from the House and President Donald Trump.

The action comes amid a weeks-long funding standoff over DHS which oversees the TSA.

The prolonged partial government shutdown has disrupted airport security leading to staffing shortages and longer wait times at airport checkpoints.

According to a senior administration official, the payments will be funded using money from Trump’s 2025 tax and spending bill.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Trump’s signature to appear on U.S. currency, a first for a sitting president

OpenAI ad pilot crosses $100M annualized revenue mark in six weeks

Pentagon weighs sending up to 10,000 more troops to the Middle East: report

President Donald Trump has extended a pause on U.S. strikes targeting Iran's energy infrastructure, pushing the deadline to April 6 as diplomatic efforts continue between Washington and Tehran.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 1.3% at $95. Bitcoin is down 1.6% at $67,000. Gold is up 1.4% at $4,442.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.4% and the DAX is down 1%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Unity Software (U) +15% – Shares surged after the company raised its FQ1 outlook.
